title: Simplified Theoretical Analyses of Lagging Darcy Flow and Land Subsidence in a Groundwater Pumping System of Three Horizontal Layers: An Elastic Thin Plate Approach

research.question: Can delayed groundwater flow and elastic aquitard bending explain and quantify pumping-related deformation in a three-layer aquifer-aquitard system?
research.system: An infinite axisymmetric sequence of a pumped lower confined aquifer, a leaking elastic aquitard represented as a thin plate, and an unpumped upper confined aquifer around a constant-rate fully penetrating line-sink well.
research.inputData: Dimensionless parameter experiments and 24-hour constant-rate pumping-test drawdowns from observation wells at San-Tan and Ta-Hsin on Taiwan's Choshui River alluvial fan.
research.method: Couple radial layer continuity and vertical aquitard leakage to a flux-lag/storage-lag Darcy law in the pumped aquifer and thin-plate equilibrium, solve by Laplace-Hankel transformation, rank parameter influence with Morris analysis, and infer six parameters by Bayesian calibration.
research.benchmark: The solution is compared with Neuman-Witherspoon, Lin-Yeh, Wang, Yu, and Theis limits that selectively omit lag, bending, or leakage; calibrated drawdown is compared with two pumping tests.
research.uncertainty: Morris elementary effects rank global parameter influence. MCMC posteriors report medians and 95% credible intervals under homoscedastic Gaussian drawdown error, but pumping-well storage and skin are excluded and the deformation stress test is synthetic.
research.conclusion: The model reproduces observation-well drawdown, but inferred plate-bending displacement at both sites is below routine geodetic detection; millimeter-scale displacement appears only in a deliberately altered synthetic parameter regime.

narrative.throughline: Carry a delayed pumping signal through three hydraulic layers into an elastic plate, then ask whether the resulting bend is large enough to observe.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: Classical Darcy diffusion transmits hydraulic forcing instantly through its constitutive law, while field subsidence may persist after pumping changes.
narrative.stakes: Attributing delayed land motion to plate bending requires separating hydraulic lag from leakage, storage, and sediment compaction.
narrative.resolution: The coupled solution fits hydraulic observations but predicts undetectably small field-site bending, sharply limiting what this elastic mechanism alone can explain.

background.premise: Persistent post-pumping subsidence motivates a model containing hydraulic time lag and overburden flexure.
background.visibleEvidence: Published observations report subsidence persisting for weeks to years after pumping reductions.
background.whyNow: Classical diffusion or consolidation models used alone omit one or both mechanisms emphasized by the paper.
background.boundary: The study isolates elastic aquitard bending and lagging flow rather than simulating inelastic consolidation.
background.locator: PDF page 1, Abstract; PDF page 1, Introduction, first paragraph
background.sourceExcerpt: system or consolidation models struggle to reproduce this lingering response because they omit hydraulic time lags
background.visualObject: pumping-reduction marker followed by a lingering-subsidence duration band
background.motionPurpose: Establish the reported persistence that motivates the coupled model.
background.stepDetail: Mark a pumping reduction and extend only the reported subsidence-duration band.
background.transitionBridge: Represent the two physical delays explicitly.

background.premise: Lagging flow distinguishes an inertial pause before flux responds from delayed release out of poorly connected pores.
background.visibleEvidence: A hydraulic-gradient pulse reaches independently labeled flux-lag and storage-lag clocks with no imposed duration ordering.
background.whyNow: Separate lag parameters can reshape early and intermediate drawdown and bending.
background.boundary: Lagging behavior is assigned only to the pumped aquifer; the aquitard and upper aquifer remain Darcy media, and no fixed tau_q-versus-tau_s ordering is assumed.
background.sourceExcerpt: a lagging formulation is adopted, introducing independent flux-lag
background.visualObject: gradient pulse splitting into flux-lag and storage-lag pathways
background.motionPurpose: Separate momentum delay from delayed pore-water release without ranking the two fitted lag times.
background.stepDetail: Animate the flux response and pore-water release on independent clocks without ordering their durations.
background.transitionBridge: Embed those delays in the lower aquifer of a three-layer system.

background.premise: The hydro-mechanical object is a pumped aquifer, an intervening elastic aquitard, and an unpumped upper aquifer.
background.visibleEvidence: Three horizontal layers surround a line-sink well, with radial lower-aquifer flow, vertical leakage, and plate deflection.
background.whyNow: Pressure differences across the aquitard create the load that bends the plate.
background.boundary: The layers are homogeneous, isotropic, laterally infinite, and time invariant; deformation is small, linear elastic, and axisymmetric.
background.sourceExcerpt: aquifer, (ii) an elastic aquitard, and (iii) an upper unpumped con-
background.motionPurpose: Fix the scientific object that every later equation and result modifies.
background.stepDetail: Label each layer, place the line sink in the lower aquifer, and show leakage crossing the plate.
background.transitionBridge: Write conservation in each layer before coupling mechanics.
background.minimalText: Pumped aquifer | elastic aquitard | upper aquifer

methods.input: Layer drawdowns, aquifer storage, radial discharge, and vertical leakage across aquitard interfaces.
methods.validity: Radial flow is used in the aquifers and vertical flow in the aquitard; recharge and external loads are excluded but could be superposed in the linear framework.
methods.sourceExcerpt: unpumped aquifer. For each layer, a radial continuity equation is
methods.visualObject: three layer-control volumes with radial and vertical arrows
methods.motionPurpose: Show where pumped water, aquifer storage, and leakage enter each balance.
methods.stepDetail: Pulse the lower line sink, contract radial storage rings, and transfer leakage between adjacent layers.
methods.transitionBridge: Replace the lower-aquifer flux closure with its delayed form.

methods.input: Lower-aquifer hydraulic gradient and distinct flux- and storage-lag times.
methods.output: Delayed lower-aquifer discharge coupled to drawdown storage.
methods.validity: The first-order Taylor approximation assumes lag times are small relative to the characteristic time scale; only the pumped aquifer uses this relation.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Classical Darcy’s law assumes an instantaneous proportionality be-
methods.visualObject: hydraulic-gradient and discharge arrows separated by two lag clocks
methods.motionPurpose: Delay the constitutive response before it enters the layer balances.
methods.stepDetail: Advance the gradient pulse, then animate discharge and storage release at their respective lag times.
methods.transitionBridge: Convert the resulting cross-aquitard head difference into plate load.

methods.input: Drawdown difference between lower and upper aquifers and aquitard flexural rigidity.
methods.output: Axisymmetric aquitard deflection in response to pumping w(r,t).
methods.validity: The aquitard is a linearly elastic thin plate with small deflection; inertia and membrane forces are neglected.
methods.sourceExcerpt: aquitard in response to pumping can be written as
methods.visualObject: elastic aquitard bending under a cross-layer pressure load
methods.motionPurpose: Turn a hydraulic head contrast into the paper's mechanical observable.
methods.stepDetail: Shade the pressure difference, apply it to the plate, and deform the plate axisymmetrically toward the well.
methods.transitionBridge: Transform the coupled differential system for rapid evaluation.

methods.input: Dimensionless coupled drawdown and plate equations with initial and boundary conditions.
methods.output: Laplace-domain coupled radial drawdown and deflection equations.
methods.validity: The zero initial drawdown and deflection conditions support the time transform of the coupled system.
methods.locator: PDF page 10, Appendix Solution Derivation of Equation 19, Equation A.1
methods.sourceExcerpt: Applying the Laplace transform to Eq. (16), the dimensionless flow
methods.visualObject: time-dependent radial fields folding into Laplace-domain radial profiles
methods.motionPurpose: Remove the time derivatives while retaining the unresolved radial coordinate.
methods.stepDetail: Show the zero initial state; sweep through time; accumulate the transformed response; retain radial profiles for each layer.
methods.transitionBridge: The radial dependence is removed by the Hankel transform in M05.

methods.input: Laplace-domain coupled radial drawdown and deflection equations.
methods.output: Algebraic drawdown and deflection solutions in Laplace-Hankel space.
methods.validity: Axisymmetric radial geometry admits the Hankel transform used in the solution derivation.
methods.locator: PDF page 10, Appendix Solution Derivation of Equation 19, Equations A.4-A.5
methods.sourceExcerpt: Then, the Hankel transform is employed to eliminate the parameter
methods.visualObject: Laplace-domain radial profiles resolving into Hankel modes and algebraic layer amplitudes
methods.motionPurpose: Replace radial derivatives with transform modes without implying a return to physical space.
methods.stepDetail: Project each radial profile onto Hankel modes; remove the radial derivative; couple the layer amplitudes; reveal the algebraic system.
methods.transitionBridge: The spatial transform is inverted numerically in M06.

methods.input: Algebraic drawdown and deflection solutions in Laplace-Hankel space.
methods.output: Laplace-domain drawdown and deflection profiles in physical radius.
methods.validity: The paper identifies numerical Hankel inversion as the route from Hankel modes back to radial profiles.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.1.5, paragraph following Equations 19-20
methods.visualObject: Hankel modes recombining into radial layer profiles while a Laplace marker remains visible
methods.motionPurpose: Restore physical radius without yet resolving physical time.
methods.stepDetail: Hold the Laplace coordinate fixed; weight the Hankel modes; sum their radial contributions; reveal the layer profiles.
methods.transitionBridge: The remaining Laplace coordinate is inverted in M07.

methods.input: Laplace-domain drawdown and deflection profiles in physical radius.
methods.output: Physical-time radial drawdown and aquitard-deflection predictions.
methods.validity: The paper specifies the Stehfest scheme for numerical inversion of the Laplace-domain solutions.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.1.5, paragraph following Equations 19-20
methods.visualObject: Laplace-domain radial profiles unfolding into physical-time drawdown and plate-deflection traces
methods.motionPurpose: Resolve the final transform coordinate into the predictions used by sensitivity and field analysis.
methods.stepDetail: Apply the Stehfest weights; advance physical time; reveal drawdown curves; reveal the corresponding plate displacement.
methods.transitionBridge: The physical predictions are sampled for Morris sensitivity analysis in M08.

methods.input: Baseline and one-at-a-time perturbed outputs for eight sampled dimensionless parameters.
methods.output: An elementary-effect value for each parameter perturbation and selected time.
methods.validity: The Morris method uses a ten-level grid and 20 trajectories, totaling 180 model evaluations.
methods.sourceExcerpt: an elementary effect is defined as:
methods.visualObject: one sampled parameter coordinate displaced beside its resulting model-output change
methods.motionPurpose: Convert each one-at-a-time perturbation into an elementary effect.
methods.stepDetail: Hold the baseline output, displace one parameter coordinate, and divide the resulting output change by the sampling interval.
methods.transitionBridge: Aggregate the elementary-effect magnitudes in M09.

methods.input: Elementary-effect values for one parameter across the Morris trajectories.
methods.output: Mean absolute elementary effect mu_i^* for each parameter and selected time.
methods.validity: The mean absolute elementary effect is the average magnitude of model-output change across the parameter domain and indicates overall importance.
methods.sourceExcerpt: equals the average magnitude of the change
methods.visualObject: absolute elementary-effect magnitudes collecting into a mean influence bar
methods.motionPurpose: Summarize each parameter's overall influence without mixing in effect spread.
methods.stepDetail: Take the magnitude of each elementary effect and average those magnitudes into mu_i^*.
methods.transitionBridge: Calculate the spread of the same elementary effects in M10.

methods.input: Elementary-effect values and their mean for one parameter across the Morris trajectories.
methods.output: Elementary-effect standard deviation sigma_i for each parameter and selected time.
methods.validity: The standard deviation measures elementary-effect spread; a large value indicates nonlinear behavior or parameter interactions.
methods.sourceExcerpt: represents the spread of the elementary effects
methods.visualObject: elementary-effect distribution widening around its mean with a sigma_i spread marker
methods.motionPurpose: Separate nonuniform influence from the absolute-mean importance measure.
methods.stepDetail: Measure each elementary effect's deviation from the mean and combine the squared deviations into sigma_i.
methods.transitionBridge: Use the sensitivity results to interpret parameters inferred from field drawdown in M11.

methods.input: Observation-well drawdown from 24-hour San-Tan and Ta-Hsin pumping tests.
methods.output: Posterior medians and 95% credible intervals for each site.
methods.validity: The likelihood assumes homoscedastic Gaussian errors; 36 walkers run 3000 steps with 1000 burn-in steps, and pumping-well data are excluded because storage and skin are absent from the forward model.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Posterior summaries are reported as medians with 95% credible intervals
methods.visualObject: six site-specific posterior parameter distributions with median markers and 95% credible-interval spans
methods.motionPurpose: Tie the theoretical mechanism to field hydraulic evidence without pretending the pumping well is represented.
methods.stepDetail: Sample parameter sets, retain the parameter draws, and mark each distribution with its median and 95% credible interval.
methods.transitionBridge: Use the inferred field parameter sets as inputs to the plate-bending simulation in M12.

methods.input: Field posterior parameter sets and a separate deliberately altered parameter scenario.
methods.output: Sub-detection field-site displacements and 8-15 mm synthetic stress-test displacements after one year.
methods.validity: The millimeter response is not a field estimate: it follows a synthetic 100-fold reduction in transmissivity and aquitard conductivity, 100-fold increase in skeletal storage, and tenfold reduction in rigidity.
methods.sourceExcerpt: To illustrate conditions under which the bending-lag mechanism becomes observable, a single synthetic stress test was performed
methods.visualObject: field posterior displacement traces beside a separately labeled synthetic detectability trace
methods.motionPurpose: Separate inferred field magnitude from an intentionally stressed detectability regime.
methods.stepDetail: Keep field traces below the detection band, then change only the listed parameters and reveal the synthetic millimeter trace.
methods.transitionBridge: Return to the three-layer field object and state the mechanism's explanatory limit.

symbols.meaning: radial drawdown in the pumped or upper aquifer
symbols.transition: Layer drawdowns become a cross-aquitard pressure contrast.

symbols.meaning: flux-lag and storage-lag times in the pumped aquifer
symbols.transition: The clocks delay discharge and pore-water release in M02.
The plate profile becomes the displacement trace used for detectability.
Reducing D softens the synthetic stress-test plate.

symbols.meaning: mean absolute elementary effect for parameter i
symbols.transition: The mean-influence bar gains a spread marker in M10.

symbols.meaning: standard deviation of elementary effects for parameter i
symbols.transition: Sensitivity importance and spread guide interpretation of inferred parameters.

return.visualObject: the original three-layer well cross-section with six site-specific parameter posterior distributions, an almost-flat field aquitard, and a separately ghosted synthetic detectable bend
return.claim: Elastic plate bending under the parameter posteriors inferred from San-Tan and Ta-Hsin observation-well drawdown remains below routine geodetic detection.
return.claimBoundary: The 8-15 mm response belongs only to the synthetic stress test, not either field site. The elastic plate-lag mechanism excludes inelastic consolidation and cannot by itself explain centimeter-scale basin subsidence.
return.locator: PDF pages 8-10, Section 3.4, Figures 11-12; PDF page 9, Conclusions
return.sourceExcerpt: component remains small: the median end-of-year subsidence equals

visual.primaryObject: three horizontal aquifer-aquitard layers around a line-sink pumping well
visual.accentObject: elastic aquitard plate and its detectability threshold
visual.measuredQuantity: observation-well drawdown and predicted plate deflection
visual.geometryNotes: Use axisymmetric radial distance r, radial flow in both aquifers, vertical leakage through the intervening aquitard, a fully penetrating lower-aquifer line sink, and small vertical plate deflection. Keep field posterior and synthetic stress-test scenes visually distinct.
